title: Introduction to ARM Assembly
summary: ARM (Advanced RISC Machine) is the most widely deployed instruction set architecture. Assembly language is the lowest level of human-readable programming, with near 1-to-1 mapping to machine instructions.
content_html:
What is Assembly Language?

Assembly language is the lowest level of human-readable programming. Each instruction maps directly to one machine instruction (a bit string the processor executes). The assembler translates ASM text into binary machine code.

Why Learn Assembly?

Use CaseDescription
Tiny / fast programsNo OS overhead, maximum performance
Hardware driversDirect register and peripheral access
Compiler writingUnderstand what high-level code compiles to
Security researchReverse-engineer binaries and find vulnerabilities
Bare-metal programmingRun code without an OS on embedded hardware

RISC vs CISC

PropertyCISC (e.g. Intel x86)RISC (e.g. ARM)
Instruction complexityComplex multi-cycle instructionsSimple single-cycle instructions
Transistors forComplex instruction decoderMore registers and cache
Code sizeSmaller codeLarger code
Power usageHigherLower
Best suited forDesktop/server performanceMobile, IoT, embedded systems

ARM Architecture

ARM = Advanced RISC Machine
130 billion ARM processors produced. Used in mobile devices, routers, Raspberry Pi, Arduino, tablets, and most IoT devices. About 100 instructions in total.

Programming Language Hierarchy

LevelLanguageExample
HighestHigh-levelPython, Java, JavaScript
MidSystems languageC, C++, Rust
LowAssemblyARM ASM, x86 ASM
LowestMachine codeBinary bit strings
ARMlite Simulator: ARMlite (by Peter Higginson) simulates a cut-down 32-bit ARM processor with visual memory and display output. Used in this course to focus on ARM concepts without physical hardware.

exercises.title: ARM acronym
exercises.options_json: ["Advanced Register Machine", "Advanced RISC Machine", "Arithmetic Register Module", "Automated RISC Microcontroller"]

exercises.title: RISC property
exercises.options_json: ["Complex multi-clock instructions", "Simple single-clock instructions", "Large instruction decoders", "High power usage"]

exercises.title: Assembler role
exercises.options_json: ["C code to Python", "Assembly text to binary machine code", "Binary to hexadecimal", "High-level code to assembly"]
exercises.answer: Assembly text to binary machine code

exercises.title: ARM use case
exercises.prompt: ARM processors are best suited for:
exercises.options_json: ["High-end desktop gaming", "Data centre servers", "Mobile and IoT devices", "Supercomputers"]

exercises.title: Assembly level
exercises.options_json: ["Highest level of human-readable code", "Lowest level of human-readable code", "Same as machine code", "Same as C code"]

exercises.title: ARM instructions
exercises.prompt: Approximately how many instructions does the ARM instruction set have?

exercises.title: Bare-metal programming
exercises.options_json: ["Programming with an OS", "Programming without an OS, direct hardware access", "Programming in Python", "Programming with virtual memory"]
exercises.answer: Programming without an OS, direct hardware access

exercises.title: RISC power
exercises.prompt: RISC architectures typically use less power because:
exercises.options_json: ["They have fewer registers", "Simpler instructions require fewer transistors and clock cycles", "They run at lower frequencies", "They have no cache"]
exercises.answer: Simpler instructions require fewer transistors and clock cycles

exercises.title: Assembly reverse engineering
exercises.prompt: Assembly knowledge is useful for security research because:
exercises.options_json: ["Assembly code is always commented", "All compiled binaries can be disassembled to ASM", "ASM is identical to C", "Assembly has built-in security features"]
exercises.answer: All compiled binaries can be disassembled to ASM
